meta.title: Automated Code Review: Building a 3-Axis Quality System
meta.excerpt: How we evolved from ad-hoc code review to a systematic 3-axis model covering code quality, security, and architecture. Includes the full checklist, severity classification, and lessons from 10 reviews.

props.content: After 10 code reviews of this project, we refined our review process from "check everything and hope for the best" to a structured 3-axis system. Each axis targets a different risk category, uses different tools, and catches different classes of defects.

Our first reviews were unstructured -- we checked whatever came to mind, missed patterns, and frequently re-discovered the same issues in later sessions. The 3-axis model emerged from categorising every finding we had made and realising they fell into exactly three buckets: code quality, security, and architecture.

before.title: Ad-Hoc Review (Reviews 1-5)
items: No checklist -- different things checked each time
No clear 'done' criteria for a review

after.title: 3-Axis Review (Reviews 6-10)
items: Structured checklist per axis with clear criteria
Findings logged with IDs for tracking
Clear completion criteria: all 3 axes checked

props.content: Each axis is independently valuable and independently runnable. A small CSS change only needs Axis 1. A new server action needs Axes 1 and 2. A new page needs all three. Match the review depth to the change scope.
Code quality catches technical debt, readability issues, type safety violations, and dead code. These are the easiest to automate and the most common findings.

Security catches XSS vectors, injection points, missing validation, and exposed secrets. These findings are rarer but far more dangerous -- a single security issue can compromise the entire application.

Security findings are always treated as Critical or High severity. Unlike code quality issues that degrade gradually, a single security vulnerability can be exploited immediately. Never defer security findings to "when convenient."

Architecture catches structural issues: broken navigation, stale labels, import resolution failures, and convention violations. These are the hardest to automate but catch the most impactful issues -- a broken nav link means an entire page is unreachable.

Every finding gets a severity level that determines the response. This prevents the common anti-pattern of treating all findings equally and either fixing everything (burnout) or fixing nothing (accumulating debt).

rows: Rate limiting bypass, exposed API keys

features.title: Critical + High = 25%
features.description: Only 17 of 67 findings were Critical or High. But these 17 findings represented 80% of the risk.

features.title: Medium + Low = 75%
features.description: 50 findings were Medium or Low. These were batched into maintenance sprints without urgency.

steps.title: Step 1: Automated Scanning
steps.description: Run grep-based checks for all three axes in parallel. Catches 60-70% of findings in minutes. Each axis has a defined set of grep patterns and cross-reference queries.

steps.title: Step 2: Manual Deep Review
steps.description: Focus on areas automated scanning cannot cover: business logic correctness, component composition patterns, error handling completeness, performance implications.

steps.title: Step 3: Finding Documentation
steps.description: Log every finding with: unique ID, severity, description, file location, and resolution status. This creates a searchable history for pattern detection.

steps.title: Step 4: Resolution Sprint
steps.description: Fix findings in severity order (Critical first). Verify EACH fix individually before moving to the next. Never batch fixes without per-fix verification.

props.content: Lesson from Review 6a: We once tried to fix all findings in a single sprint without verifying between fixes. Three fixes introduced new bugs that were not caught until the next review. Now we verify after every individual fix -- the extra time is negligible compared to the cost of cascading errors.

rows: Batch fix sprint introduced 3 new bugs

props.content: Each review made the process better. The system is self-improving: every new finding type gets added to the checklist, every new failure mode gets a recovery protocol, and every process mistake gets a prevention rule.

points: The 3-axis model works because each axis is independently valuable and independently runnable
Match review depth to change scope: CSS change = Axis 1 only, server action = Axes 1+2, new page = all 3
Severity classification prevents both over-reaction (fixing everything) and under-reaction (fixing nothing)
Always verify fixes individually -- never batch without per-fix verification
The review system is self-improving: every finding type gets added to the checklist for future detection
